---
title: "Sentiment Analysis"
---

## What Is Sentiment Analysis?

Sentiment analysis assigns emotional valence — positive, negative, or neutral — to text. In media research it is used to study:

- Tone of news coverage towards politicians or policies
- Audience emotional responses in comments or social media
- Changes in public mood over time

> **Caveat**: Dictionary-based sentiment methods assume that sentiment is carried at the word level and is context-independent. Irony, negation, and domain-specific language all pose challenges.

## Lexicon-Based Approaches

### AFINN

Words scored −5 (very negative) to +5 (very positive).

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

get_sentiments("afinn") |> slice_sample(n = 10)
```

### Bing

Binary positive/negative classification.

```{webr}
library(tidytext)

get_sentiments("bing") |>
  count(sentiment)
```

### NRC

Eight emotions (anger, fear, anticipation, trust, surprise, sadness, joy, disgust) plus positive/negative.

```{webr}
library(tidytext)

get_sentiments("nrc") |>
  count(sentiment, sort = TRUE)
```

## Applying Sentiment to a Corpus

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

headlines <- tibble(
  date = as.Date(c("2024-01-10","2024-02-14","2024-03-05",
                   "2024-04-22","2024-05-01","2024-06-15")),
  text = c(
    "Economy surges as unemployment falls to record low",
    "Government slammed over corruption scandal and cover-up",
    "Hope for climate deal as nations reach historic agreement",
    "Crisis deepens with fresh allegations against the prime minister",
    "Breakthrough vaccine offers new hope in disease fight",
    "Violence erupts at protest; dozens injured in clashes"
  )
)

sentiment_scores <- headlines |>
  mutate(line = row_number()) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = "word") |>
  group_by(date) |>
  summarise(sentiment = sum(value))

ggplot(sentiment_scores, aes(date, sentiment, fill = sentiment > 0)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("tomato", "steelblue")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Sentiment score (AFINN)", title = "Headline sentiment over time") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Emotion Analysis with NRC

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

text <- tibble(
  text = paste(
    "The prime minister faces growing anger over the betrayal of public trust.",
    "Thousands of citizens expressed fear and sadness at the announcement.",
    "Opposition leaders expressed disgust but offered hope for a better future."
  )
)

text |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("nrc"), by = "word") |>
  filter(!sentiment %in% c("positive", "negative")) |>
  count(sentiment, sort = TRUE) |>
  ggplot(aes(n, reorder(sentiment, n), fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Word count", y = NULL, title = "Emotion profile (NRC lexicon)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Validation

Always validate sentiment scores against human judgements for your specific domain. General-purpose lexicons trained on product reviews may perform poorly on political news.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Example validation workflow
# 1. Sample 100–200 documents
# 2. Have two coders assign sentiment manually
# 3. Compute inter-rater reliability (Cohen's κ)
# 4. Compare automated scores to human judgements (correlation, accuracy)
library(irr)
kappa2(human_ratings)  # from irr package
```

## Further Reading

- @liu2012sentiment provides a comprehensive overview.
- @young2012affective assess sentiment lexicons for political science.
